articles.title: Surprise hike in international student visa application fees “a direct hit to Australia’s competitiveness”
articles.summary: Australia’s Department of Home Affairs announced an immediate, unexpected increase in international student visa application fees, effective 1 July 2026, with no prior consultation with the education sector. The fee hike applies to study, work, and working-holiday visas and is non-refundable, affecting all sub-sectors of the international education industry.
articles.insight:
This unexpected fee hike signals potential downward pressure on Australia’s attractiveness for cost-sensitive students, particularly from price-sensitive markets like India and Nigeria, though its full impact on global demand remains uncertain amid broader economic and geopolitical factors influencing student mobility.  
Admissions teams should monitor application volume trends from key feeder countries to Australia over the next 6–12 months and assess whether counterpart institutions in the UK, USA, and New Zealand see correlated shifts in inquiry patterns or conversion rates.

articles.title: ICEF Podcast: “Good, steady, and disciplined”: New Zealand’s plan for sustainable international enrolment growth
articles.summary: The ICEF Podcast features hosts Craig Riggs and Martijn van de Veen discussing recent developments in international student recruitment, including new foreign enrolment figures from the Netherlands and Azerbaijan’s goals to expand its international student base. The episode then includes a live discussion with ICEF’s Rod Hearps focused on New Zealand’s strategy for achieving sustainable international enrolment growth, described as “good, steady, and disciplined.”
articles.insight:
New Zealand’s “good, steady, and disciplined” approach signals a deliberate shift toward quality-controlled growth rather than volume-driven expansion, potentially influencing global perceptions of stability and regulatory predictability—though direct impacts on demand in other English-speaking destinations remain uncertain without parallel policy shifts.  
Admissions teams should monitor New Zealand’s visa processing timelines, post-study work rights adjustments, and enrolment caps by sector or nationality, as these may foreshadow similar recalibrations elsewhere or reveal emerging preferences among students prioritising long-term residency pathways.
